content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 23, 2017


David M. Snow
Assistant City Attorney
City of Beverly Hills
455 N. Rexford Drive, Room 230
Beverly Hills, California 90210

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-16-268

Dear Mr. Snow:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Beverly Hills Planning
Commissioner Andrew Licht regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that this advice does not analyze any other area of law, including
Section 1090, the Public Contract Code, or common law conflicts of interest. Please also note that
we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any
advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

        Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating
to a proposed luxury condominium building project (the "Project") because the Commissioner
leases and subleases office space in close proximity to the Project?

CONCLUSION

        No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the
Project because those decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
the Commissioner's financial interests based on the facts presented.

FACTS

        You are the Assistant City Attorney for the City of Beverly Hills and the authorized
representative of Planning Commissioner Andrew Licht.


___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-268
Page No. 2

The City's Planning Commission will soon decide whether to recommend that the City
Council approve, and issue a Planned Development Permit for, a proposed project for the
construction of a luxury condominium building (the "Project"). The Project would be five stories
tall, and include 26 residential units and between 3,000 to 4,000 square feet of ground floor retail or
restaurant space. The Project site has been vacant for several years but was formerly the site of the
Friars Club of Beverly Hills.

The neighborhood surrounding the Project is established and economically vibrant, includes
a variety of commercial uses and multi-family residential housing, and is already subject to high
residential density and commercial activity. The Project site is just south of the Beverly Hilton and
Waldorf Astoria hotels. There are restaurants, retail establishments, and offices to the north, east,
and west of the site. And there is multi-family residential housing to the south of the site.

You state that the Draft Environmental Impact Report (DEIR) for the Project concluded that
the Project would not result in any significant impacts if the City imposes the mitigation measures
identified therein, and that it is the City's general practice to impose all mitigation measures
identified in environmental impact reports along with other conditions of approval deemed
necessary and appropriate. There is street parking available near the Project site, and according to
the Planning Commission Report on the Project dated September 8, 2016, the Project would include
one level of underground parking, providing a total of 74 parking spaces for residents and guests.

Because the Project site is currently zoned C-3A, a commercial zone that does not allow
multi-family residential uses but does permit office use, the Project applicant also seeks approval of
amendments to the City's General Plan and Zoning Ordinances necessary to construct the Project.
The Planning Commission cannot grant final approval to the General Plan or zoning amendments,
but instead will forward recommendations on those issues to the City Council. The Planning
Commission has the authority to make final decisions on Planned Development Permits, but those
decisions are appealable to the City Council.

The Commissioner leases office space in a building that is approximately 497 feet from the
proposed Project site, and the lease for that office space runs through the end of April 2019. The
leasehold is worth in excess of $2,000. The Commissioner has leased the office space for
approximately 23 years through various leases over that time. The proposed amendment of the
City's General Plan and Zoning Ordinances would only apply to the Project site, not the
Commissioner's leased office space.

The Commissioner operates a motion picture production business and a real property
management business out of the leased office space. The Commissioner owns, and has invested in
excess of $2,000 in, both of those businesses. The Commissioner's real property management
business manages both commercial and residential real properties.

The Commissioner also subleases portions of the office space in close proximity to the
Project to two other businesses, Michael Silber's business that manufactures souvenir bobblehead
figurines and Marilyn Atlas's talent agency that represents actors, animators, and writers.

You provided additional facts on February 3, 2017.

File No. A-16-268
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests identified in that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the Commissioner's interests implicated by the decisions relating to the Project and the facts presented are the following:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) The Commissioner has business interests in his motion picture production business and real property management business because he owns, and has invested in excess of $2,000 in, each of those businesses.

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) The Commissioner has a real property interest in his leased office space because the underlying lease is worth more than $2,000.

• Any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The Commissioner has source of income interests in Michael Silberd and Marilyn Atlas, respectively, because they are subtenants of his leased office space.

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. (Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

The Commissioner's interests are not explicitly involved in decisions regarding the Project.³ Therefore, if the financial effect of the decisions at issue can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Business Interests

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's interest in a business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably

___________
² Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if a decision affects a business entity or real property in which the official has an interest, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business entity interests, or Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests. Because the decisions at issue affect the Commissioner's business and real property interests, we apply the materiality standards applicable to those interests, and we do not further address the Commissioner's interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family members.

³ An interest is explicitly involved if it is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. If an interest is explicitly involved the effect of the decision is presumed foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(a).)

File No. A-16-268
Page No. 4

foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held
business entity at issue.

The Project may lead to greater residential density and commercial activity within the
immediate area surrounding the Project site and the Commissioner's leased office space. However,
the profitability of either of the Commissioner's businesses is not reliant on, or closely related to,
those factors, and there is no indication that increased activity in the immediate area from the
Project will affect either business. The greater neighborhood surrounding the Project site and the
leased office space is established and economically vibrant, includes a variety of commercial uses
and multi-family housing, and is already subject to high residential density and commercial activity.
The DEIR for the Project concluded the Project would not result in any significant impacts if the
mitigation measures identified therein are imposed, and the City's general practice is to impose all
mitigation measures along with other conditions of approval deemed necessary and appropriate.
Therefore, the effect of the decisions relating to the Project would not contribute to a change in the
value of the Commissioner's motion picture production or real property management businesses.

Real Property Interest⁴

Regulation 18702.2(b) provides that a decision's effect on any real property in which the
official has a leasehold interest is material if the decision will: (1) change the termination date of the
lease; (2) increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the
rental value of the property, and the official has a right to subleae the property; (4) change the
official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property; or (5) impact the official's use and
enjoyment of the real property.

The decisions relating to the Project will not change the termination date of the
Commissioner's lease. The amendments to the City's General Plan and Zoning Ordinances would
only apply to the Project site, and therefore, the decisions would not change the Commissioner's
actual or legally allowable use of the office space. And, considering the nature of the property and
the character of the existing neighborhood, there is no indication that the decisions will increase or
decrease the rental value or potential rental value of the office space, or impact the Commissioner's
use and enjoyment of it. Thus, the decisions relating to the Project would not have a material
financial effect on the Commissioner's real property interest in the leased office space.

Source of Income Interests⁵

Regulation 18702.3 provides that, for income received by an official for goods and services
provided in the ordinary course of business, a decision's effect is material if the official knows or
has reason to know that the source of income has an interest in a business entity that will be
financially affected under the materiality standards applicable to a business entity.

⁴ We only analyze the Commissioner's real property interest in the leased office space at issue because you
have not asked about, or provided facts relevant to, any of the Commissioner's other property interests.

⁵ We only analyze the Commissioner's source of income interests in Mr. Siber and Ms. Atlas because you
have not asked about, or provided facts relevant to, any of the Commissioner's other source of income interests.

File No. A-16-268
Page No. 5

As noted above, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on a business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity at issue.

Similar to the Commissioner's businesses discussed above, the profitability of Mr. Silber and Ms. Atlas's respective businesses is not reliant on, or closely related to, any increased residential density or commercial activity within the immediate area of the Project site and the leased office space potentially resultant from the Project. Furthermore, the surrounding neighborhood is already subject to high residential density and commercial activity, the DEIR concluded that the Project would not have any significant impacts if the mitigation measures identified therein are imposed, and the City's general practice is to impose all identified mitigation measures along with other conditions of approval deemed necessary and appropriate. Therefore, the effect of the decisions relating to the Project would not contribute to a change in the value of Mr. Silber or Ms. Atlas's respective businesses.

Accordingly, we conclude that the Act would not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the Project because the decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Commissioner's financial interests based on the facts presented.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to a proposed luxury condominium building project (the "Project") because the Commissioner 
leases and subleases office space in close proximity to the Project?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Project because those decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
the Commissioner's financial interests based on the facts presented.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otheIWise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any 
position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) The Commissioner has business interests 
in his motion picture production business and real property management business because he 
owns, and has invested in excess of $2,000 in, each of those businesses. 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(b ). ) The Commissioner has a real property interest in his leased office 
space because the underlying lease is worth more than $2,000. 
• Any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months 
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The Commissioner has source of income interests in 
Michael Silber and Marilyn Atlas, respectively, because they are subtenants of his leased office 
space. 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. 
(Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.2 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The Commissioner's interests are not explicitly involved in decisions regarding the Project.3 
Therefore, if the financial effect of the decisions at issue can be recognized as a realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 1870 I (b ). ) 
Business Interests

business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably

foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to a proposed luxury condominium building project (the "Project") because the Commissioner 
leases and subleases office space in close proximity to the Project?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Project because those decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
the Commissioner's financial interests based on the facts presented.
